title: Quranic Duas: The Supplications Allah Preserved in His Own Revelation
excerpt: Every dua preserved inside the Quran carries a unique weight — it is a prayer Allah chose to keep for all humanity, for all time. Explore Quranic supplications in Arabic with multiple translations and their deep meanings.
author: QuranBookk
content:
## A Prayer That Allah Chose to Preserve Forever

The Quran contains thousands of commands, stories, descriptions, and laws. It also contains something rarer: **supplications** — prayers that prophets made to Allah in their moments of greatest need, which Allah chose to preserve in His own revelation.

Think about what that means. Of all the words ever spoken in prayer throughout human history, these specific supplications are the ones Allah deemed worthy of inclusion in His eternal Book — the ones He preserved for every generation of believers until the Day of Judgement. A dua that Allah preserved in the Quran is not just a prayer. It is a prayer with divine endorsement.

The [QuranBookk Quranic Duas collection](/quran/ayats/) brings every one of these supplications together — with full Arabic text, multiple translations, and their profound meanings.

## The Prophets' Crisis Prayers — And What Happened After

### Yunus (AS) in the Darknesses
In one of the most dramatic moments in Prophetic history, Prophet Yunus (ﷺ) was swallowed by a whale, in darkness upon darkness — the darkness of the ocean, the darkness of the whale's belly, the darkness of his own distress.

His prayer:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="لَّآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنتَ سُبْحَٰنَكَ إِنِّى كُنتُ مِنَ ٱلظَّٰلِمِينَ"
  translation="There is no deity except You; exalted are You. Indeed, I have been of the wrongdoers."
  reference="Surah Al-Anbiya 21:87"
/>

Allah's response is recorded immediately: *"So We responded to him and saved him from distress. And thus do We save the believers."* (21:88)

This dua is considered by scholars to be among the most powerful in the Quran for moments of extreme distress — because the response is stated categorically: Allah saved him, and thus He saves the believers.

### Ayyub (AS) After Years of Suffering
Prophet Ayyub (ﷺ) endured prolonged illness and loss that lasted years. His prayer was brief:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="أَنِّى مَسَّنِىَ ٱلضُّرُّ وَأَنتَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ"
  translation="Indeed, adversity has touched me, and You are the Most Merciful of the merciful."
  reference="Surah Al-Anbiya 21:83"
/>

Notice what this prayer does not contain: no specific request. No demand. Just an acknowledgement of suffering and an invocation of Allah's mercy. Sometimes the most complete dua is simply telling Allah what has touched you, and naming who He is.

### Musa (AS) Before the Challenge of His Life
When Prophet Musa (ﷺ) was sent to confront Pharaoh — the most powerful ruler of his age — his prayer was for ability, not for outcomes:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="رَبِّ ٱشْرَحْ لِى صَدْرِى وَيَسِّرْ لِىٓ أَمْرِى وَٱحْلُلْ عُقْدَةً مِّن لِّسَانِى يَفْقَهُوا۟ قَوْلِى"
  translation="My Lord, expand for me my breast and ease for me my task and untie the knot from my tongue that they may understand my speech."
  reference="Surah Taha 20:25-28"
/>

For any Muslim about to speak in a high-stakes situation — a job interview, a difficult conversation, a presentation, a dawah encounter — this is the dua of the Prophet who faced a throne and spoke truth.

### The Rabbana Duas: The 40 Supplications Beginning with "Our Lord"

The Quran contains approximately 40 supplications that begin with *Rabbana* (رَبَّنَا) — *Our Lord*. These are among the most universally known and recited Islamic prayers, covering nearly every human situation:

- *Rabbana atina fid-dunya hasanatan...* — For goodness in this world and the next (2:201)
- *Rabbana la tuzigh qulubana...* — For protection from hearts that deviate (3:8)
- *Rabbana zalamna anfusana...* — The prayer of Adam and Hawwa after their descent (7:23)
- *Rabbana hab lana min azwajina...* — For righteous spouses and children (25:74)
- *Rabbana la tuhammiln ma la taqata lana bih...* — For relief from unbearable burden (2:286)
- *Rabbana taqabbal minna...* — The prayer of Ibrahim and Ismail as they built the Kaaba (2:127)

<Callout type="tip">
The Rabbana duas are ideal for a structured dua practice: select one Rabbana dua per day, memorise it in Arabic, understand its meaning, and recite it in your daily prayers. Over 40 days, you will have memorised a complete collection of Quranic supplications covering the full range of human need.
</Callout>

## Using the Quranic Duas Collection

The [QuranBookk Quranic Duas collection](/quran/ayats/) organizes every Quranic supplication with:
- Full Arabic text in the standard Uthmani script
- Multiple translations for complete meaning comprehension
- The verse reference (Surah and verse number)
- Context of the dua within the broader narrative

For the duas of specific prophets, pair the collection with [Ask Islam AI](/ask-islam-ai/) — ask about the context and story behind any dua to understand not just the words but the moment that produced them.

For building a complete personal dua practice, combine the Quranic Duas with the [Digital Tasbih](/digital-tasbih/) — the Tasbih for counting Dhikr, the Duas collection for the supplications that accompany it.

For exploring the 99 Names of Allah referenced in these duas — *Anta Ar-Rahman, Anta Al-Ghaffar* — the [99 Names of Allah feature](/99-names-of-allah) provides the full meaning and context of each Name.

## The Prayers That Have Always Been Heard

Every Quranic dua was answered — that is how it entered the Quran. The prayer of Yunus was answered. The prayer of Ayyub was answered. The prayer of Musa was answered. The prayer of Ibrahim was answered. These are not historical prayers from long-gone prophets. They are living prayers, preserved for you — because the situations that produced them are human situations that recur in every age.
